data.title: Southwest Airlines Could Fly High On Demand Growth, But Needs To Improve Margins
data.description:
Ahead Of Spring/Summer Flying Season, One Airline Stands Out

In this holiday-shortened trading week due to President's Day, I'm taking to the skies again figuratively, this time in today's article covering Dallas-based Southwest Airlines (NYSE:LUV), who just had their last earnings results a few weeks ago on Jan. 30th.

This airline, paying a 2.3% dividend yield as of this article writing, and boasting "a total fleet of 803 Boeing 737 aircraft and served 117 destinations," according to its stock page, was also in the news last week when an article in Travel Weekly mentioned its introduction of overnight red-eye flights as well as "the introduction in 2026 of assigned seating and extra legroom seats, as well as a series of cost-cutting measures."

My thesis today explores the future upside potential of this stock and the downside risk to consider, the sustainability of the dividend, as well as comparing a few metrics to similar airline peers.

Thesis: Keep Holding On, With Seatbelts Fastened For Now

My thesis today will argue that the prior hold rating I gave this stock in 2023 should be reaffirmed again, as it has not made a strong case for either buying or selling.

Its upside potential is driven by positive factors such as macro-level expected demand growth for flights and air travel as well as being 3rd in US airline market share, having a low leverage risk and declining debt, and driving global growth opportunities with its recent partnership with Icelandair.

However, its downside risk comes from lack of proven positive cash flow growth lately, lack of proven dividend growth, a profit margin behind key peers, and overvalued vs key airline peers, as well as the risk of over-reliance on an exclusively Boeing fleet.

The following sections go into these topics in more detail, with supporting evidence.

Macro Demand Picture For Airlines in 2025 Seems Favorable

To set the backdrop for this sector, here is a chart showing the airline leaders by US market share, according to the Bureau of Transportation Statistics, with Southwest being 3rd in market share:

I think that Southwest having such a large market share in the US, and a robust route network with so many destinations, should position it to take advantage of the expected air travel demand growth in 2025.

According to a senior columnist for Yahoo! Finance in late December, data from one of the major airline trade associations at the time pointed to air travel growth expected in 2025:

Passenger numbers are expected to reach 5.2 billion in 2025, up 6.7% from this year and exceeding the 5 billion mark for the first time, according to a forecast out this week from the International Air Transport Association (IATA).

Coupled with this macro data, the airline itself said in its FY24 Q4 results commentary in late January that they estimate RASM (revenue per available seat mile) to grow 5% - 7% in FY25 Q1, which is more positive news, I think.

Next, let's talk more about some revenue and earnings drivers specific to this airline.

New Global Partners Could Drive Revenue Growth, But High Costs Keep Weighing on Profits

What I believe could be a driver of upside to revenue growth, besides the introduction of new red-eye flights and assigned seating I mentioned earlier, is to look towards the global airline market for synergies.

For instance, from the recent 10K report commentary, "Icelandair will become Southwest's initial partner with an expected launch on February 13, 2025. Southwest intends to add at least one additional partner carrier in 2025."

Consider, for example, that such global airline network partnerships could drive more international travelers to Southwest's vast route of US destinations, as you see in the route map below, which could make it more competitive vs large network carriers like American (AAL) and Delta (DAL) who also serve many of those same cities.

The market seems to have noticed Southwest's growth ambitions, and we can see from Seeking Alpha peer data that fwd revenue growth is expected to beat both Delta and American, while coming close to that of United.

However, where Southwest lags behind peers is in profit margin. For example, both its gross profit margin and its net income margin significantly falls behind the sector average.

On a more granular level, Southwest lags behind several peers, including Delta, United and Alaska Air Group (ALK) when it comes to profitability metrics:

Investors will want to see that the company has a plan in place to tackle the high overhead costs of running an airline, and become more cost-efficient.

From what I found in its 10K commentary, the airline expects its 2025 CASM (cost per available seat mile) "to increase in the range of 7 percent to 9 percent, year-over-year, driven primarily by the continuation of inflationary pressures."

However, costs "are expected to improve throughout the year as labor comparisons ease, efficiency initiatives generate modest capacity growth, and cost plan benefits are aggressively pursued."

Cash Flow Growth Could Use Improvement

Positive cash flow growth is one of the business fundamentals I have been tracking in recent articles, as I believe it supports a stronger dividend sustainability case, too.

When it comes to Southwest, it seems to be struggling in this regard.

For example, it has faced negative YoY growth in operating cash flow, while its overall sector has seen positive growth.

The drivers of this on the cash flow statement seem to have been the quarters ending March 2024 and Jun 2024, which saw negative cash from operations driven in part by net losses.

Further, the company has not made the case with FCF/share, with 5 of the last 6 reported quarters having a negative FCF/share.

Having painted a picture of the cash flow situation, next let's talk somewhat about the dividend sustainability and growth potential for this stock, since I picked this stock in part for being a dividend payer.

A Low-Yield Dividend Stock Lacking in Proven Dividend Growth

With a dividend yield of 2.3%, it beats its sector average yield however I would not call it a high-yield stock necessarily, if that is your goal solely, since I can find much higher yields in the 5 - 10% range among some REITs I covered lately, for example.

Because many dividend investors expect growth of future dividend income, so far, this airline has not made the case that it can be a strong dividend grower, as you can see in the YCharts below, as this sector clearly took a hit during the 2020-2021 pandemic travel slump and likely had to save cash:

Even though the airline industry has generally recovered by 2023 and beyond that, Southwest has not hiked its dividend past $0.18/share since 2023, according to history.

This lack of a steady dividend growth history, along with a trend of negative free cash flow/share, could hurt the case for dividend investors to run to this stock, and so I would call this a slight downside factor.

Declining Long-Term Debt, And Investment-Grade Credit Rating

Being in an industry like airlines which carries such high overhead and capital expenses, we should consider the amount of leverage this airline has compared to peers.

From the YCharts I created below, which tracks the 3-year trend for debt/equity ratio, Southwest remains on the low end of this peer group with a D/E of 0.64, which has been rather steady. The outlier in this group is really American Airlines, with its negative equity.

Notable to mention also about Southwest, when looking at its long-term debt trend from Jun 2023–Dec 2024, it has reduced its LT debt by 37% in this time ($7.9B down to $5B), which is impressive and indicates an airline with much lower leverage risk now, which, I think, is especially beneficial in high interest rate environments when debt costs more.

In addition, another positive factor is that major credit rating agency Fitch has reaffirmed its investment-grade "BBB+" rating on Southwest Airlines after a January review:

I believe the combination of the relatively low D/E ratio, the declining trend in LT debt, and the strong credit rating from Fitch will drive upside as these factors will attract investors looking for stocks with lower leverage risk.

Share Price Growth Since 2023, But Still Trading Near Average And Mostly Undervalued

Since my original coverage of this stock in Oct 2023, when I said to hold on to it longer, it has gone up about +26% as of this article writing.

What drove my hold rating at that time was a positive revenue outlook but offset by profitability growth struggles at the time.

Here is where the stock is today, compared to its 200-day SMA and 5 year high/low:

With the share price hovering around its 200-day simple moving average, and remaining on the lower end of its 5-year range, my question is whether this presents a buy opportunity now, or a continued hold, but also perhaps a sell now that it has gone up since 2023.

To get a more holistic view, I also considered valuation data, which showed that this stock is trading at an 18.5x forward earnings multiple, much lower than the sector average of 21.98 fwd P/E (GAAP). It is also undervalued vs its sector when it comes to fwd EV/EBITDA, and trailing price/book value.

However, to get more granular, let's also look at the forward earnings multiple vs key airline peers mentioned earlier, and that actually shows the stock much more overvalued vs them.

In this case, we can say this stock presents a mixed valuation picture, being undervalued vs the sector but overvalued when comparing to specific airlines that have leading market share in the US.

I believe the market is expecting this airline to achieve a nice turnaround since those losing quarters last year, and we can see 11 upward EPS revisions so far and a consensus estimate +73% YoY EPS growth, so such a positive forecast combined with the low valuation vs sector could be an upside as it attracts value-driven investors.

This will depend on further cost reductions to improve profitability, I think, since the top-line growth case is there.

Additional Risk: Despite Top Service Scores, Reliance Solely On Boeing Aircraft Is Concerning

In this section, we want to touch on an additional downside or upside risk not mentioned yet, but that could have a business impact and therefore relevant to investors.

Because several airlines in the US often serve the same routes, there is competition for those passengers, and one key differentiator can be service.

Many years ago, while working for a managed solutions provider that runs contact centers for foreign airlines in the US, an airline CEO gave me sage advice about passengers that whether they are calling in or whether they are on board, passengers will remember how you may them feel.

With that said, one upside driver for the Southwest brand is further investment in the customer experience from start to finish.

In its 10K report recently, the company mentioned, for example, investment in "contact center service modernization, airport service modernization, Customer Service communications, and disruptions management."

Notable also to mention from its Q4 results is that Southwest was "ranked the #1 airline for economy class customer satisfaction in the J.D. Power 2024 North America airline satisfaction study for the third consecutive year."

These facts, I think, could help Southwest to drive the passenger brand experience, in a crowded and competitive route market.

However, a key disruptor, I think, of the airline experience... and I know this having traveled by air for many years... is aircraft being grounded or severely delayed due to consistent technical issues. This airline's over-reliance on a single aircraft in its portfolio, the Boeing 737, and such a large fleet of this type, may pose a future downside risk if there is a massive grounding of this type due to a technical issue found.

Not only does that mean lost flights but also a logistical logjam and lots of angry passengers.

For instance, in the wake of a door panel flying off during flight on an Alaska Airlines Boeing 737 Max last year, the Federal Aviation Administration stepped up its oversight of Boeing (BA), with Reuters reporting last fall that the "audit of Boeing completed in February found 97 incidents of noncompliance, spanning issues in Boeing's manufacturing process control, parts handling and storage, and product control."

So, even though Southwest has top scores in customer satisfaction, its over-reliance on Boeing and lack of fleet diversification beyond Boeing to other manufacturers such as Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) and Embraer (ERJ) could pose a future downside risk.

Conclusion Reaffirms Prior Hold Rating Again

To summarize the upside and downside factors I presented in today's article, consider the following simplified rating sheet, which shows that total upside and downside actually offset each other, thereby justifying that I reaffirm my prior hold rating on this stock.

Although my thesis this time considered additional angles not discussed originally, it led to the same conclusion again for the reasons I listed above.

Wrap-Up: Adding Airlines To Dividend Ideas

This article follows my ongoing theme on this platform for 2025 called Financial Markets with Albert Anthony, where I try to find quality dividend paying stocks to grow a diversified dividend portfolio.

To join the discussion in reader comments, I welcome your thoughts on the question: What factors do you think will contribute to Southwest hiking its dividend in the future, or why they might not?

data.title: Upstart: Strong Buy Rating Ahead Of Projected $1 Billion Revenue In 2025
data.description:
Following 2024, when the stock of Upstart Holdings (NASDAQ:UPST) appreciated 55%, the lending platform reported stunning Q4 earnings on Tuesday, obtaining a strong three-day performance of approximately 22%, bringing it to its highest level since May 2022.

The big surprise came with the normalized EPS results. The market expected Upstart to have a negative EPS of $-0.04 for the quarter, but it achieved an EPS of $0.26. This is the first time Upstart has achieved normalized profitability after six quarters of losses, and the market loved that.

At the same time, revenues came in far above expectations, with $218.96 million in revenue from the $181.92 million expected, representing a surprise of more than $37 million or 20.37%.

In addition, the guidance provided for 2025 brought most of the positive sentiment on the stock. For 2025, management expects to achieve $1 billion in revenue for the first time since LTM Q3 of 2022. This implies a potential revenue growth rate acceleration from 24% in 2024 to 57% in 2025 and an adjusted EBITDA from 2% to 18%. This guidance far exceeded analysts' expectations of $823.1 million in revenue and an adjusted EBITDA margin of 13%.

Finally, the company expects to at least break even full-year GAAP profitability, which hasn't occurred since 2021, when Upstart achieved $135.4 million in net income and the stock was trading far above its current level.

Loan Growth

The top-line returns were achieved thanks to soaring demand for all types of loans. Personal lending, which accounts for most of the loans, had $2,038 million in transaction dollars, growing at 65% YoY. Auto lending grew 224%, and the newly introduced home equity lines of credit (HELOCs) saw a sequential growth of 59%.

Based on what CFO Sanjay Datta mentioned during the conference call, the soaring loan demand arrived through a mix of a more accurate model, half of which he believed came from that and a better credit environment that has allowed for lower APRs on the platform.

Model 19

Recently, Upstart launched Model 19, allowing intermediate delinquencies (30-90 days past due) to be considered training data. This means loans that recover to good standing become useful data for developing a more robust model. In the previous models, this wasn't the case, as it only considered the terminal state and timing of a loan in the training data set and not what happens in between.

At the same time, management also mentioned that they have invested in model calibrations on its core model, and based on backtesting, the calibrated model would've avoided 55% of excess loan defaults.

Lending

The key advantage of Upstart is that its revenue comes primarily from commissions on underwriting loans with the capital of bank and credit union partners. Only a small percentage of the loans are held by Upstart, which allows for the scalability of the loan origination. Therefore, for the lending platform, it is essential to secure enough commitments to meet loan demand in order to profit and obtain commission revenue.

In Q4, originations with lending partners grew 76% year over year, with 30% of that growth coming in the last quarter of the year. Upstart also increased commitments by $1.3 billion, refinanced convertible debt, and raised $500 million to improve their liquidity in anticipation of their expected growth in 2025.

Valuation

For a company that has trailing unprofitable while having a market cap of $7.87 billion, almost every valuation metric will make it look overvalued. At the same time, due to not owning most of the loans, its light balance sheet gives it an expensive price to book of 12.17x.

Morgan Stanley Sell → Hold $12 → $70 14-Feb-25
Citigroup Strong Buy $87 → $108 13-Feb-25
Goldman Sachs Strong Sell $14 → $15 12-Feb-25
BofA Securities Sell $33 → $39 12-Feb-25
Needham Strong Buy $100 → $108 12-Feb-25
B. Riley Securities Hold → Strong Buy $49 → $105 12-Feb-25
J.P. Morgan Sell → Hold $57 → $79 12-Feb-25
Piper Sandler Buy $85 → $105 12-Feb-25

Source: Stock Analysis

Therefore, it is best to rely on the consensus estimates of sell-side analysts. Here, analysts were bullish in updating their price targets on Upstart's stock after Q4 earnings. The median price target among eight analysts is $92, representing an upside potential of approximately 11.6%.

Risks

Although the outlook for 2025 looks spectacular due to the guidance provided, Upstart is a company highly dependent on the credit cycle. The more loan demand, the more commissions they earn, and the better the business does.

Yet, even though Upstart's competitive advantage comes from its technology based on alternative risk models, its revenues are highly volatile and not upward trending, as in a classical high-growing software company. Therefore, this is a company whose stock is likely to experience high degrees of volatility going forward until the company finds other revenue sources that are less than perfectly correlated to the credit cycle.

Takeaway and Stock Recommendation

Upstart's guidance and performance speak well about what could happen next with the stock price. Considering factors such as an improved model and sell-side analysts' ratings, I will rate UPST as a "strong buy" as the conditions are set for the company to do well in 2025.

data.title: LyondellBasell: All Eyes Are On Dividend Safety In 2025
data.description:
LyondellBasell Industries (NYSE:LYB) stock has experienced yet another challenging year in 2024 as the cyclical downturn in the petrochemical industry continues with full force.

Over the past 12-months alone, LYB fell by more than 20% and is now trading at levels last seen at the equity market bottom during the early days of the pandemic.

Consequently, LYB's dividend yield is now approaching 7%, which is the highest level on record if we disregard the temporary drop of the share price in early 2020.

What all that means is that LYB is now looking increasingly attractive for two distinct groups of investors - dividend investors seeking for high yields and contrarians looking to bet on the cyclical swing within the sector.

While I will focus on the former group here, the recent remarks by LYB management highlight the extent to which the macroeconomic headwinds have intensified in recent years.

There is no getting around it, 2024 was another challenging year for petrochemicals. (...) Across our key businesses, fourth quarter industry margins are about 60% of historical averages, underscoring the depth of the current downturn.

Source: LYB Q4 2024 Earnings Transcript

Pricing of key products has fallen sharply since 2021 and is now just slightly above the pre-pandemic levels of 2019.

At the same time, however, costs are materially higher following the recent surge in inflation and the level at which the oil prices have settled.

Based on all that, LYB margins are now significantly lower than their historical averages.

As one of the largest and highest margin players in the space, LYB is likely to be in a good position to reward investors who are willing to wait for demand for durable goods to recover and with that, prices to normalize.

However, the market is also worried that there are some important structural shifts in the industry relative to prior cycles. Namely, the slower growth globally and particularly in China, the higher energy costs, and regulatory changes in Europe. All that makes the notion that LYB margins would return to their prior cyclical highs a highly uncertain assumption for investors.

That is why, my investment thesis is now primarily focused on around LYB's dividend, and I am less willing to speculate on any potential cyclical upswing in favour of current investors. With a dividend yield of nearly 7% the only issue is just how safe the dividend payments are and what's the probability of a dividend cut in the coming years.

Focusing On Dividend Safety

One of the main starting points when evaluating LYB's dividend safety is what the company's management has been signalling over the past year through their capital allocation decisions.

In May of last year, LYB's management has made the decision to increase the dividend by an impressive 7%, which was the 14th consecutive annual increase. The reason why I use the word "impressive" is not so much because of the actual rate of increase, but rather due to the extremely high payout ratio on a GAAP basis (more on that later).

On top of all that, LYB's management is also increasing the cash allocated to capital expenditure, which is yet another strong signal of their conviction in the business turnaround.

For fiscal year 2024, Capex stood $1.5bn, which was around 112% of the annual depreciation expense. The outlook for FY 2025 is now for total Capex to reach $1.9bn, which is a meaningful increase from the prior period and well-above the sustaining levels.

When we look at GAAP figures for the past year, all that might sound as nonsense given the fact that LYB's net income has fallen below the annual dividend payments.

One-off Items

Once we exclude the one-off expenses for 2024 (identified items) then LYB's net income of $2.1bn is sufficient to cover the recently increased dividend payments, but the question of long-term sustainability remains.

The net income adjustments were related to lower of cost or market (LCM), gain on sale of business, asset write-downs and refinery exit costs with most of them occurring for the second year in a row, which could be a cause of concern when relying on adjusted figures.

By far the largest item, however, was related to asset write-downs in LYB's European assets during 2024 which is related to the company's strategic review within the region. These are non-cash items and as such bear no weight on the dividend safety, but it should be mentioned that the review process is still ongoing, which is likely to result in more write-downs or restructuring expenses in 2025.

The fourth quarter included identified items of $852 million net of tax. The items included non-cash write-downs related to our O&P Europe, Asia and International and Advanced Polymer Solutions segment of $769 million and $42 million, respectively, and costs incurred from plans to exit the refining business.

Source: LYB Q4 2024 Earnings Transcript

Planned Maintenance

In comparison to 2024, international operations should benefit from no planned maintenance in 2025, with the first quarter likely to be impacted by certain supply constrains in Germany.

In contrast to 2024, our O&P EAI segment has no major turnaround scheduled for the coming year. Nevertheless, feedstock supply for one of our German crackers is currently constrained due to unplanned downtime at a nearby refinery. With those constraints and ongoing soft market demand, we expect to operate our European assets at a rate of 75% during the first quarter.

Source: LYB Q4 2024 Earnings Transcript

But even after factoring these feedstock supply issues, European assets are expected to be operated at a rate of 75%, in comparison to 65% during the last reported quarter of 2023.

During the quarter, European markets remained weak with softer seasonal demand and lower consumer confidence. Polymer prices were modestly higher with an improved sales mix and stable naphtha feedstock costs. Due to the low demand, we operated our assets at rates of approximately 65% during the quarter.

Source: LYB Q4 2023 Earnings Transcript

The first quarter EBITDA would also be impacted by planned maintenance in the Olefins & Polyolefins Americas segment, with downtime being heavily weighted towards the first half of the year.

Refining

Yet another reason why Q1 of 2025 could be a rather unusual period in comparison to the rest of FY 2025 is the expected exit from the refining business. This is likely to be associated with certain one-off expenses, but the business will no longer be a headwind for profitability as it was in 2024.

Investor Takeaway

Following yet another disappointing year for LyondellBasell shareholders, the focus is shifting toward the highly attractive dividend yield of nearly 7%. After adjusting for non-cash items and taking into account expected operational developments in 2025, it appears that the dividend is safe for the time being. Margins are likely to gradually improve in 2025 and LYB's management confidence in terms of capital allocation further supports that.

data.title: The Kraft Heinz Surprise: Outshining Defensive Packaged Foods Over Next 5 Years
data.description:
One stock that has lagged the U.S. equity market rather badly over the last 10 years is The Kraft Heinz Company (NASDAQ:KHC). Amazingly, the quote has declined, despite the S&P 500 roughly tripling in price. Kraft Heinz was formed in July 2015 through a major merger, using the existing Heinz security as the combination entity. Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B) has and continues to own a rather large stake, 325.6 million shares worth $10 billion currently. On the risk side of the equation, the business has been straddled with too much debt, preventing the stock price from rising materially. This is the quick-take summary to contemplate when buying/owning shares.

The good news is Kraft Heinz has slowly been tackling its debt problem, although the process has played out over many years. Today, the average retail investor and Wall Street analyst has kind of left the company for dead, as an investment with little "future" upside. However, I strongly disagree.

In fact, the latest share price selloff has opened a terrific 5.5% dividend yield, with very low valuations on the business being ignored by all. Believe it or not, Kraft Heinz has actually "outperformed" most all of the big food stocks over the past five years, and the next five look even more promising. Compared to peers/competitors General Mills (GIS), J. M. Smucker (SJM), Mondelez (MDLZ), PepsiCo (PEP), Hershey (HSY), Coca-Cola (KO), Campbell's Company (CPB), McCormick (MKC), Tyson (TSN), and Danone S.A. (OTCQX:DANOY), Kraft Heinz has absolutely held its own for shareholder value on a total return basis.

Measured from the $28 intraday quote reached last week, I am modeling a double in the stock price over the next five years to $56 is entirely possible, which would work out to a compounded gain of +20% annually when you include the dividend. In a technology-bust scenario, where the S&P 500 and NASDAQ 100 indexes proceed to flatline over the next five years (similar to the Dotcom bubble bursting after the year 2000), Kraft Heinz may actually morph into a must-own defensive stock for the patient, independent-thinking investor.

Let's review some of the logic to buy Kraft Heinz under $30. If you receive 5.5% in dividend payouts each year (a higher rate than available from cash savings or Treasury bond rates), while you pocket even minor price appreciation, "future" defensive-minded Kraft Heinz performance could prove a top choice with general Wall Street indexes trading at record overvaluations in February 2025.

Debt Ratios Improving

On a 10-year chart back to pre-merger Heinz shares, we can review how net financial debt (debt minus cash holdings) rose substantially on the merger in July 2015. Peaking near $31 billion in 2018, net debt is now down to $18.5 billion (at the end of December 2024). Even better news is operating cash flow is running at a rate of 5x total interest expense, up considerably from 3x in 2021 and less than 1x in early 2018. So, today's debt backdrop is the least risky since the merger commenced.

Debt to equity (40%) and total assets vs. liabilities (44%) are also near or at the lowest readings post-merger.

Cash flow to debt calculations have steadily improved over time. In this case, higher numbers showcase less debt risk overall. Rising from 0.1x in 2020 to 0.2x currently, Kraft Heinz has moved from the worst setup in the large-cap packaged food universe to a relatively average group operating condition over the last five years.

Further, cash flow to sales of 16.2% is a terrific margin. Kraft Heinz remains one of the leading food giants for margins on sales.

Favorable Valuation Setup

If new to Kraft Heinz as an investor, you would guess the valuation would be somewhere in the middle of the peer group, with financials improving over the last 10 years. You would have guessed incorrectly. Instead, Wall Street has moved on from KHC, while largely forgetting the bullish reasons to own it.

For example, the easily sustainable forward 2025 P/E is just 10.4x on cash earnings (adjusted from GAAP accounting). The packaged food leaders have a median average around 15x today.

When we include debt and cash holdings, an enterprise valuation can be compared/contrasted with the same zeroed-out debt setup as peers. The forward EV to EBITDA number of 8.7x is quite a distance from the 11x group median average.

EV to forward estimated sales is not a huge discount to the industry, but the 2.1x ratio is below average.

When reviewing a long list of fundamental data, Seeking Alpha's computer-ranking system gives the company an overall "A-" Quant Valuation Grade. Compared to its 5-year trading history and current industry valuations, Kraft Heinz qualifies as a much stronger choice for value investors than the credit it receives.

Cash Returns

Perhaps the most bullish Kraft Heinz datapoints to consider are the raw cash returns available for investors. The 8.57% trailing "free" cash flow return is the best in the large-cap packaged food sector. If you are hunting for a company with high rates of cash flow on your investment dollar to pay dividends, fix its balance sheet, and eventually grow the business again, KHC scores quite well.

The real-world cash distribution yield of 5.53% on a trailing basis is also the best upfront dividend rate in the major food peer group. The median average is closer to 3.5%.

But that's not all for the income investing crowd to get excited about. The current dividend yield is also 4.77x the equivalent rate from the S&P 500 index. Against 1.2% in average U.S. equity market yield for the blue-chip universe, Kraft Heinz is providing its best relative yield since the 2015 merger.

Final Thoughts

Wall Street analysts are very bummed about Kraft Heinz's future, projecting the recent past for results again in 2025, 2026, 2027. KHC has become the Rodney Dangerfield of the stock market, a company getting no respect.

My forecast is for cash EPS to rise better than 5% annually over the next five years from a little under $3 in 2025, partly riding higher-than-expected inflation rates.

Simplified, around $4 billion in cash flow yearly is the 2024-25 story. $1 billion is spent on necessary capital expenditure, and $2 billion is sent out as dividend checks, leaving $1 billion for discretionary management reinvestment. I would focus on repaying debt as my first priority. In five years, interest rates will be higher than today, as my view. Why refinance debt maturing at rising expense?

Additionally, if $5 billion were cut off the $18.5 billion net-debt reading today, Wall Street would undoubtedly pull the valuation of the business from one of the lowest-multiple operations up to the middle of the peer pack. For example, $13.5 in net debt by 2030 with annual cash flow of $5 billion (works out to 2.7 years of theoretical debt repayment to zero) would look far more conservative than 2024's $18.5 billion in debt divided by $4 billion in cash flow (4.6 years).

If Kraft Heinz can hike product sales with inflation and reduce net interest expense, cash EPS of $3.75 per share seems more credible for an estimate by 2030. Given a normalized P/E of 15x for the major food group, I am modeling $56 for a share quote in 60 months. And, this number with small dividend raises will likely deliver a still decent 3% to 3.5% dividend yield at that time, for new equity buyers.

It's honestly been a game of attrition for Kraft Heinz, working to pay down debt levels since late 2015. The company has sold off divisions, restructured operations, laid off workers, and spent as little as possible on CAPEX. My opinion is the next five years will prove much easier on the business, in terms of creating sales and EPS growth, with debt settings closer to industry averages in 2025.

What are the downside risks? I think my main worries are macroeconomic in nature. For instance, a stock market crash or prolonged bear market would affect the share quote to a degree. Even with a trading history of robust outperformance during sharp Wall Street drawdowns (for both Kraft Foods and Heinz before the merger), I would not expect KHC to experience significant gains in price under such a scenario. Plus, a deep recession (with Trump shaking the economy to its foundation, while U.S. debts are spiraling out of control) could mean sales and earnings struggle to maintain their current level.

However, with Berkshire Hathaway helping to run the business (with its 26.9% ownership stake), I am not overly concerned about management execution. Kraft Heinz has a very competitive position in the packaged food market, owning some of the top brands in the world. If you can exclude the debt load from your thinking, who doesn't want to own a high free cash flow and dividend yield machine, with great operating margins and consistent consumer staple demand for its products?

I rate Kraft Heinz shares a Buy under $30 for a 12-month outlook, and I am looking to enter a decent-sized position over the next few weeks.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
